segments.title: Conwy's maritime castle plan
segments.summary: The opening uses Conwy's town port, castle port, promontory, and sea access to show why the castle was built as a maritime stronghold.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke starts with a diorama because Conwy's layout makes the sea-power argument visible at once. The town has its own port, the castle has a separate waterside access point, and the key town spaces sit under the castle's fire. The result is a fortress whose control problem cannot be separated from naval supply and local movement by water.

evidence.note: The transcript introduces Conwy Castle and highlights the town port, beach, fishing ships, and separate castle port.
The transcript explains that key town areas were under castle fire, the castle sat on a sea-wrapped promontory, and it could not be blocked from resupply without a navy.

segments.title: Attack geometry at the gate
segments.summary: The plan view explains Conwy's eight towers, outer and inner wards, drawbridge approach, right-hand turn, and crossfire problem for attackers.
segments.body: This watch point is a compact lesson in how castle plans turned architecture into tactical danger. Dr. Clarke walks through the drawbridge approach, the forced turn at the gate, and the way towers exposed attackers to fire from multiple directions. Even a successful entry into the outer ward would only begin another fight through the inner defenses.

evidence.note: The transcript introduces the castle plan, outer and inner wards, eight towers, and the difficult drawbridge approach.
The transcript describes the turn into the gate, crossfire from towers, and the need to fight through both outer and inner wards.

segments.title: Welsh site, Edwardian rebuild
segments.summary: The site history connects Welsh princely power, the River Conwy crossing, Edward I's 1282 campaign, abbey relocation, and the abandonment of Deganwy Castle.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke places Conwy on an older Welsh political and religious landscape before turning to Edward I's rebuilding program. The site had links to a Cistercian monastery and Welsh princely structures, but its river crossing made it strategically valuable to Edward after the 1282 campaign. The new town and castle replaced earlier arrangements, including the relocation of the abbey and the abandonment of Deganwy Castle.

evidence.note: The transcript describes the pre-English site, monastery, Welsh princely associations, town-wall remains, and native Welsh construction.
The transcript explains the river crossing, Edward's 1282 campaign context, the decision to build a new town and castle, abbey relocation, and Deganwy Castle's abandonment.

segments.title: Construction as royal logistics
segments.summary: Conwy's building program is presented as a managed logistics effort, with royal control, John Bonvillars, Master James of St George, seasonal labor, and high cost.
segments.body: The construction section shows the castle as an organized state project rather than a vague medieval building effort. Edward kept direct control, with John Bonvillars managing day-to-day works and Master James of St George overseeing the wider construction program. The labor system drew seasonal workers through Chester and by sea, while the combined castle and town walls cost about 15,000 pounds.

evidence.note: The transcript outlines Edward's direct control, John Bonvillars, Master James of St George, the curtain walls, towers, interior buildings, and town walls.
The transcript describes recruited laborers massing at Chester or arriving by sea and gives the roughly 15,000 pound cost of the castle and town walls.

segments.title: Small garrison, sea relief
segments.summary: The garrison and 1294-95 siege show how a small professional core, local support, and sea supply made Conwy hard to reduce.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke notes that Conwy's charter linked castle and town authority, with the constable also serving as mayor and overseeing a professional garrison of about thirty. That force could be reinforced by local troops and militia, so the permanent guard was only part of the defensive picture. During Madog ap Llywelyn's revolt, Edward was besieged at Conwy until relief arrived by sea, while the castle was supplied by water throughout.

evidence.note: The transcript describes the constable-mayor relationship, the 30-soldier garrison, support staff, reinforcements, town militia, and William de Cicon as first constable.
The transcript describes Madog ap Llywelyn's revolt, Edward's siege at Conwy, sea relief in February 1295, continuing sea supply, and Edward sharing wine with the garrison.

segments.title: Later captures and decline
segments.summary: The later-history section follows Conwy through royal visits, repair cycles, a 1401 surprise capture, Civil War garrisoning, slighting, stripping, and modern repair.
segments.body: Conwy's later history shows how castles could move in and out of military relevance as politics changed. Dr. Clarke highlights repairs under the Black Prince's administration, a 1401 surprise capture by Welsh rebels posing as workmen, and a Civil War revival under John Williams, Archbishop of York. The castle was then repaired, ordered slighted, returned after the Restoration, stripped of iron and lead, and eventually restored as a preserved ruin.

evidence.note: The transcript describes Conwy's continued importance, Black Prince-era repairs, later decline, Wars of the Roses revival, and the 1401 Welsh rebel capture.
The transcript explains Civil War ownership, John Williams's royalist repairs and garrisoning, the siege, and parliamentary repairs.
The transcript covers the 1655 slighting order, Restoration return, stripping of iron and lead, ruin, and Ministry of Works repairs.

segments.title: Conwy as a war engine
segments.summary: The final assessment links Conwy's river crossing, rocky coastal ridge, imported detail stone, painted surfaces, barbican, murder holes, and longbow militia into one defensive system.
segments.body: The closing analysis explains why Conwy mattered as more than an impressive ruin. It controlled a river crossing, anchored a protected area toward the mountains, and could be defended and supplied from the sea. Dr. Clarke then ties material choices, visual status, fire risk, towers, barbican defenses, portcullises, murder holes, crossbowmen, and longbow militia into a single picture of a castle designed as an engine of war.

evidence.note: The transcript explains Conwy's river crossing, mountain-side position, sea defense, local economy, rocky ridge, quarried stone, and imported sandstone.
The transcript discusses castle appearance, status, whitewashing, and painting as both visual display and a way to make vulnerable wood harder to identify.

The transcript describes the inner and outer wards, 21-meter towers, construction holes, western barbican, early stone machicolations, portcullises, murder holes, small garrison, crossbowmen, militia, and longbow practice.
